title.en: The Fourth Crusade Sacks Constantinople
title.zh: 第四次十字军攻陷君士坦丁堡

summary.en: Diverted from its goal of Egypt, the Fourth Crusade stormed and sacked Constantinople in April 1204, shattering the Eastern Roman Empire and installing a Latin regime in the Queen of Cities.
summary.zh: 第四次十字军偏离了远征埃及的初衷，于1204年4月攻陷并洗劫君士坦丁堡，东罗马帝国就此支离破碎，拉丁政权入主这座“众城之后”。

detail.en:
In April 1204 the armies of the Fourth Crusade, entangled in Venetian debts and imperial dynastic intrigue, turned their siege engines against the greatest Christian city in the world. On 12–13 April the walls along the Golden Horn were breached, and for three days Constantinople endured a sack of appalling thoroughness: palaces and churches were stripped, the altar of Hagia Sophia broken up for its silver, ancient bronzes melted for coin, and countless relics carried off to the West — the four horses of the Hippodrome still stand in Venice.

The crusaders and Venetians then partitioned the Empire on parchment. Baldwin of Flanders was crowned Latin emperor, Venice took the harbors and islands that stitched together its maritime trade, and crusader lords carved out fiefs in Greece. The doge Enrico Dandolo, architect of the diversion, secured for Venice three-eighths of the city itself.

For the Romans the catastrophe was without precedent: the New Rome, unconquered for nearly nine centuries, had fallen not to Persians or Arabs but to fellow Christians. The Empire fractured into exile states at Nicaea, Epirus, and Trebizond, and the wound cut between the Greek and Latin churches never truly healed.
detail.zh:
1204年4月，深陷威尼斯债务与帝国王朝阴谋的第四次十字军，把攻城器械对准了基督教世界最伟大的城市。4月12日至13日，金角湾一侧的城墙被突破，君士坦丁堡随即遭受了三天彻底的洗劫：宫殿与教堂被搜刮一空，圣索菲亚大教堂的祭坛因白银被砸碎，古代铜像被熔铸成钱币，无数圣物被劫往西方——竞技场上的四匹铜马至今仍矗立在威尼斯。

十字军与威尼斯人随后在羊皮纸上瓜分了帝国。佛兰德的鲍德温加冕为拉丁皇帝，威尼斯攫取了串联其海上贸易的港口与岛屿，十字军领主则在希腊各地裂土封侯。策划这次改道的执政官恩里科·丹多洛，为威尼斯拿下了城市本身的八分之三。

对罗马人而言，这场浩劫史无前例：近九个世纪未曾陷落的新罗马，倒下时面对的不是波斯人或阿拉伯人，而是同为基督徒的西方人。帝国碎裂为尼西亚、伊庇鲁斯与特拉比松三个流亡政权，而希腊教会与拉丁教会之间的这道裂痕，从此再未真正愈合。

title.en: Theodore I Laskaris Founds the Empire of Nicaea
title.zh: 狄奥多尔一世·拉斯卡利斯建立尼西亚帝国

summary.en: Theodore I Laskaris rallied refugees from fallen Constantinople at Nicaea, founding a government-in-exile that preserved the Roman state, church, and law until the capital could be regained.
summary.zh: 狄奥多尔一世·拉斯卡利斯在尼西亚集结从沦陷的君士坦丁堡逃出的军民，建立流亡政权，为日后光复都城保存了罗马的国家、教会与法统。

detail.en:
When Constantinople fell, Theodore Laskaris, son-in-law of the emperor Alexios III, crossed to Asia Minor and gathered the fleeing aristocracy, soldiers, and clergy at Nicaea, the city of the great councils. Beating back both Latin incursions and Seljuk pressure, he forged a functioning Roman state in Bithynia; in 1208 a new patriarch, Michael IV Autoreianos, was elected in exile and crowned him emperor of the Romans, giving the refuge full constitutional legitimacy.

Nicaea was not the only claimant — the Komnenoi held Trebizond on the Black Sea and the Angeloi built a rival principality in Epirus — but Laskaris possessed what the others lacked: the patriarchate, the senate families, and an unbroken chain of imperial succession. His victory over the Seljuk sultan at Antioch on the Maeander in 1211, where he reportedly cut down the sultan in single combat, secured the eastern frontier.

The 'empire in exile' became a remarkable success: compact, defensible, well governed, and animated by a single purpose — the recovery of the City. For over half a century Nicaea kept alive the idea that the Roman Empire had not died in 1204, only withdrawn to gather its strength.
detail.zh:
君士坦丁堡陷落后，皇帝阿莱克修斯三世的女婿狄奥多尔·拉斯卡利斯渡海前往小亚细亚，在昔日举行大公会议的名城尼西亚，收拢逃亡的贵族、军人与教士。他一面击退拉丁人的进犯，一面顶住塞尔柱人的压力，在比提尼亚重建起一个运转有效的罗马国家；1208年，新任牧首米海尔四世在流亡中当选，并为他加冕为罗马人的皇帝，使这个避难政权获得了完整的法统。

尼西亚并非唯一的继承者——科穆宁家族据守黑海之滨的特拉比松，安格洛斯家族则在伊庇鲁斯另立门户——但拉斯卡利斯拥有别人所没有的东西：牧首座、元老世家，以及一脉相承的帝位传承。1211年，他在米安得河畔的安条克大破塞尔柱苏丹，据说亲手将其斩于阵前，从此稳固了东部边疆。

这个“流亡中的帝国”取得了非凡的成功：疆域紧凑、易守难攻、治理有方，且上下一心，只为一个目标——光复都城。半个多世纪里，尼西亚让世人相信，罗马帝国并未亡于1204年，它只是暂时退守，积蓄力量。

title.en: Nicaean Prosperity under John III Vatatzes
title.zh: 约翰三世·瓦塔泽斯治下的尼西亚经济繁荣

summary.en: John III Vatatzes built the Nicaean state into a model of agrarian self-sufficiency, banning luxury imports, running profitable imperial estates, and filling the treasury that would fund the reconquest.
summary.zh: 约翰三世·瓦塔泽斯将尼西亚打造成自给自足的农业国典范：禁绝奢侈品进口，经营营利的皇室庄园，为日后的光复大业充实了国库。

detail.en:
From his winter palace at Nymphaion, John III Doukas Vatatzes (r. 1222–1254) pursued an economic policy of striking deliberateness. He forbade his subjects to buy foreign luxury fabrics — 'the cloth of the Italians,' woven, he said, from the sweat of the Romans — and required the court to wear what Roman lands produced. Imperial estates were run as commercial enterprises, raising grain, vines, olives, and poultry; the emperor famously presented his empress with a coronet paid for entirely from the sale of eggs, and called it her 'egg crown.'

The results were real. While famine ravaged the neighboring Seljuk lands in the 1240s, Nicaea sold them grain at premium prices, and gold flowed east into the treasury. Taxes stayed moderate, the coinage sound, the army regularly paid; hospitals, poorhouses, and churches multiplied.

This quiet agrarian discipline was the true engine of the restoration: Vatatzes doubled his territory in Europe and Asia without financial crisis, and the surplus he accumulated made possible the campaigns that ringed Latin Constantinople. Later generations, watching the restored Empire slide into penury, remembered his reign as a lost golden age and venerated him as 'John the Merciful.'
detail.zh:
约翰三世·杜卡斯·瓦塔泽斯（1222–1254年在位）坐镇宁法昂冬宫，推行一套用心极深的经济方略。他禁止臣民购买外国的奢华织物——他说，那些“意大利人的布匹”是用罗马人的血汗织成的——并责令宫廷只穿罗马土地上出产的衣料。皇室庄园被当作商业产业来经营，种谷植葡萄、栽橄榄、养家禽；皇帝曾用卖鸡蛋的收入为皇后购置一顶冠冕，戏称之为她的“鸡蛋王冠”，一时传为佳话。

成效是实实在在的。1240年代邻近的塞尔柱领地饥荒肆虐时，尼西亚以高价向其出售粮食，黄金源源流入国库。赋税保持适度，币值稳定，军饷按时发放；医院、济贫院与教堂遍地兴建。

这种不事声张的农本之治，正是复国大业真正的引擎：瓦塔泽斯在欧亚两洲将疆土扩大一倍而无财政之虞，其积蓄的盈余支撑起对拉丁人治下君士坦丁堡的层层合围。后世之人眼见光复后的帝国日渐困窘，愈发追念他的时代如失落的黄金岁月，尊其为“仁慈者约翰”。

title.en: Michael VIII Recovers Constantinople
title.zh: 米海尔八世光复君士坦丁堡

summary.en: On 25 July 1261 the Nicaean general Alexios Strategopoulos slipped into Constantinople almost without a fight, ending fifty-seven years of Latin rule; Michael VIII Palaiologos entered in triumph and restored the Empire to its capital.
summary.zh: 1261年7月25日，尼西亚将领阿莱克修斯·斯特拉特戈普洛斯几乎兵不血刃地潜入君士坦丁堡，终结了五十七年的拉丁统治；米海尔八世·帕莱奥洛戈斯凯旋入城，帝国重归故都。

detail.en:
The recovery came almost by accident. In July 1261 Alexios Strategopoulos, patrolling Thrace with a small force, learned from local farmers that the Latin garrison and the Venetian fleet had sailed off to raid an island in the Black Sea. Guided through a postern by men who knew the walls, his soldiers seized the Gate of the Spring by night, and on 25 July the city that armies had failed to storm for decades simply changed hands. The Latin emperor Baldwin II fled by ship, leaving his regalia behind.

On 15 August, the feast of the Dormition, Michael VIII Palaiologos entered through the Golden Gate on foot, walking behind the icon of the Virgin Hodegetria, the city's ancient protectress, and was crowned anew in Hagia Sophia. The restoration was tarnished by his blinding of the boy-emperor John IV Laskaris, the legitimate heir, a crime that haunted the dynasty's conscience.

Michael found a half-ruined, depopulated capital and spent lavishly to rebuild walls, churches, and harbors. The Empire was itself again — yet smaller, poorer, and encircled by enemies, and the resources of Asia Minor that had sustained Nicaea would soon be fatally neglected.
detail.zh:
光复来得近乎偶然。1261年7月，阿莱克修斯·斯特拉特戈普洛斯率一支小部队在色雷斯巡防，从当地农人口中得知，拉丁守军与威尼斯舰队已倾巢而出，前往黑海劫掠某座岛屿。熟悉城防的向导领着他的士兵趁夜从暗门潜入，夺取了泉源之门。7月25日，这座数十年间令多少大军铩羽的城市，就这样易了主。拉丁皇帝鲍德温二世乘船仓皇出逃，连御用仪仗都弃之不顾。

8月15日圣母安息节，米海尔八世·帕莱奥洛戈斯自金门步行入城，走在城市古老的守护者——指路圣母像之后，随后在圣索菲亚大教堂再度加冕。然而这场光复蒙有污点：他刺瞎了合法继承人、少年皇帝约翰四世·拉斯卡利斯，这桩罪行日后始终萦绕在王朝的良心之上。

米海尔接手的是一座半毁而人烟萧条的都城，他不惜重金重修城墙、教堂与港口。帝国重归其位——却已更小、更穷，四面受敌；而曾经滋养尼西亚的小亚细亚腹地，不久便遭到致命的忽视。

title.en: The Union of Lyons
title.zh: 里昂教会合一

summary.en: At the Second Council of Lyons in 1274, Michael VIII's envoys accepted papal primacy to avert a new crusade against Constantinople — a diplomatic shield that the Roman clergy and people bitterly rejected.
summary.zh: 1274年第二次里昂大公会议上，米海尔八世的使节接受教宗首席权，以化解西方对君士坦丁堡的新十字军威胁——这面外交盾牌却遭到罗马教士与民众的激烈抵制。

detail.en:
The restored Empire lived under the shadow of Charles of Anjou, king of Sicily, who was openly assembling a coalition to reconquer Constantinople for the Latins. Michael VIII's counterstroke was theological: he offered Pope Gregory X the union of the churches. At Lyons in July 1274 his envoys, led by the former patriarch Germanos and the grand logothete George Akropolites, swore in the emperor's name to accept the papal primacy and the Latin creed with its Filioque clause. The pope duly forbade Charles to attack fellow Catholics, and the invasion was checked without a battle.

At home the price was ruinous. The patriarch Joseph refused the union and was replaced by the unionist John Bekkos, but the overwhelming mass of clergy, monks, and laity saw the agreement as apostasy extorted at sword-point. Michael answered resistance with imprisonment, mutilation, and exile, alienating even his own family.

The union never took root: successive popes doubted its sincerity, and in 1281 Michael was excommunicated by the very papacy he had courted. On his death in 1282 his son Andronikos II instantly repudiated the union, and Michael, savior of Constantinople, was denied an Orthodox funeral.
detail.zh:
光复后的帝国笼罩在西西里国王安茹的查理的阴影之下——他正公然纠集联军，要为拉丁人重夺君士坦丁堡。米海尔八世的反制之策却在神学层面：他向教宗额我略十世提出两教会合一。1274年7月的里昂会议上，以前任牧首格尔曼努斯和大逻各忒忒斯乔治·阿克罗波利特斯为首的使团，以皇帝之名宣誓接受教宗首席权，并承认含“和子句”的拉丁信经。教宗随即禁止查理攻打同属公教的君主，一场入侵不战而消。

然而国内代价惨重。牧首约瑟夫拒绝合一而遭撤换，由合一派的约翰·贝科斯继任，但绝大多数教士、修士与平信徒都视这纸协议为刀剑胁迫下的背教。米海尔以监禁、酷刑与流放回应反对之声，连自己的亲族也离心离德。

这次合一终究未能扎根：历任教宗怀疑其诚意，1281年，米海尔反被他苦心结好的教廷绝罚。1282年他去世后，其子安德洛尼卡二世立即废弃合一；这位君士坦丁堡的拯救者，最终连一场正教葬礼都未能得到。

title.en: Metochites, Planoudes, and the Scholars' Revival
title.zh: 梅托希特斯、普拉努德斯与学术复兴

summary.en: Around 1300 Constantinople saw a brilliant revival of learning: Maximos Planoudes edited the classics and translated Latin literature, while Theodore Metochites wrote on astronomy, philosophy, and history at the summit of state power.
summary.zh: 1300年前后，君士坦丁堡迎来学术的辉煌复兴：马克西莫斯·普拉努德斯校订古典文献并迻译拉丁名著，狄奥多尔·梅托希特斯则在权力顶峰纵论天文、哲学与史学。

detail.en:
Political decline and intellectual splendor coincided in the early Palaiologan age. The monk Maximos Planoudes assembled his great anthology of Greek epigrams, produced careful editions of Ptolemy, Plutarch, and the poets, wrote on the Indian numerals then spreading from the Arab world, and — almost uniquely among Greek scholars — mastered Latin, translating Ovid, Cicero, Boethius, and Augustine and thereby opening a window between the two halves of Christendom's learning.

Theodore Metochites embodied the ideal of the scholar-statesman. Rising to mesazon, chief minister of Andronikos II, he amassed a fortune and a famous library while composing the Stoicheiosis Astronomike, a monumental introduction to Ptolemaic astronomy, and the Semeioseis Gnomikai, essays ranging over Aristotle, historiography, and the melancholy of a state he sensed was aging. His pupil Nikephoros Gregoras carried the tradition into the next generation.

This revival — bookish, self-aware, devoted to recovering and criticizing the ancients — gave the last two centuries of the Empire their paradoxical character: a polity shrinking toward extinction while its scholars prepared, unknowingly, the classical learning they would one day carry to the West.
detail.zh:
在帕莱奥洛戈斯王朝早期，政治的衰颓与思想的璀璨恰恰同时到来。修士马克西莫斯·普拉努德斯编成规模宏大的希腊铭体诗文选，精心校订托勒密、普鲁塔克与诸诗人的著作，撰文介绍正从阿拉伯世界传来的印度数字，更几乎是希腊学者中独一无二地精通拉丁文，翻译了奥维德、西塞罗、波爱修斯与奥古斯丁，在基督教世界东西两半的学问之间开启了一扇窗。

狄奥多尔·梅托希特斯则是学者政治家理想的化身。他官至安德洛尼卡二世的首相，聚起巨富与一座名闻遐迩的藏书楼，同时写下托勒密天文学的皇皇导论《天文学基础》，以及纵论亚里士多德、史学与家国迟暮之感的随笔集《杂论》。他的弟子尼基弗鲁斯·格里高拉斯又将这一传统传至下一代。

这场复兴笃嗜书卷、极富自省，致力于重拾并评骘古人——它赋予帝国最后两个世纪一种悖论式的气质：国势日蹙、行将就木，学者们却在不知不觉间整理好古典学问，待有朝一日将其带往西方。

title.en: The Loss of Anatolia: From Bapheus to the Fall of Nicaea
title.zh: 安纳托利亚的沦丧：从巴菲乌斯到尼西亚陷落

summary.en: Between Osman's victory at Bapheus in 1302 and the fall of Nicaea in 1331, the Empire lost virtually all of Asia Minor to the Ottomans and the Turkish beyliks — the heartland that had fed and manned it for a thousand years.
summary.zh: 从1302年奥斯曼在巴菲乌斯获胜到1331年尼西亚陷落，帝国几乎将整个小亚细亚拱手让与奥斯曼人及诸突厥贝伊国——那是供养它千年的兵源与粮仓所在。

detail.en:
On 27 July 1302, near Nicomedia, a Roman force under the megas hetaireiarches Mouzalon was routed at Bapheus by the ghazi horsemen of Osman, an obscure frontier emir. The battle was small; its consequences were not. It announced that the Empire could no longer defend the Bithynian countryside, and Christian peasants began streaming toward the coasts as Osman's followers — soon called Ottomans — settled the land they raided.

The collapse was general. In the Aegean valleys the beyliks of Aydin, Saruhan, and Menteshe overran the rich cities recovered by the Laskarids; the Catalan expedition hired to reverse the tide ended in mutiny. Andronikos III and John Kantakouzenos made a last stand at Pelekanon in 1329 and were defeated. Prusa had fallen to Osman's son Orhan in 1326 and became the first Ottoman capital; Nicaea, cradle of the exile Empire, surrendered in 1331, and Nicomedia followed in 1337.

Within one generation the Empire became a European state with an Asian suburb. The loss of Anatolia's grain, taxes, and soldiers was irreversible, and across the narrow straits a new power now watched Constantinople.
detail.zh:
1302年7月27日，在尼科米底亚附近的巴菲乌斯，由禁军统领穆扎隆率领的罗马军队，被边地小埃米尔奥斯曼麾下的加齐骑兵击溃。战斗规模不大，后果却极为深远：它昭示帝国已无力保卫比提尼亚的乡野。基督徒农民开始涌向沿海，而奥斯曼的部众——不久便以“奥斯曼人”闻名——则在他们劫掠过的土地上定居下来。

崩溃是全面性的。在爱琴海诸河谷，艾登、萨鲁汗与门特谢等贝伊国席卷了拉斯卡利斯王朝收复的富庶城市；为挽回颓势雇来的加泰罗尼亚军团又以哗变收场。1329年，安德洛尼卡三世与约翰·坎塔库泽努斯在佩勒卡农作最后一搏，兵败而归。此前布鲁萨已于1326年落入奥斯曼之子奥尔汗之手，成为奥斯曼人的第一座都城；1331年，流亡帝国的摇篮尼西亚开城投降，1337年尼科米底亚继之。

仅仅一代人之间，帝国沦为一个只在亚洲留有近郊的欧洲国家。安纳托利亚的粮赋、税收与兵员一去不返，而在狭窄的海峡对岸，一个新兴强权已开始注视君士坦丁堡。

title.en: The Catalan Company Turns on the Empire
title.zh: 加泰罗尼亚军团倒戈肆虐

summary.en: Hired to fight the Turks, the Catalan Grand Company mutinied after the murder of its leader Roger de Flor in 1305, ravaging Thrace and Macedonia for years before seizing the Duchy of Athens in 1311.
summary.zh: 受雇抗击突厥人的加泰罗尼亚大军团，在首领罗杰·德·弗洛尔于1305年遇刺后哗变，蹂躏色雷斯与马其顿多年，最终于1311年夺取雅典公国。

detail.en:
In 1303 Andronikos II, desperate for soldiers, engaged some six thousand Catalan and Aragonese veterans of the Sicilian wars under Roger de Flor. The Company won real victories against the Turks in Anatolia, but plundered Roman subjects as readily as enemies, and its leader's ambitions grew alarming: Roger extracted the title of Caesar and quasi-independent power. In April 1305 he was assassinated at the court of the co-emperor Michael IX — by Alan mercenaries, with at least the connivance of the dynasty.

The Company's answer was the 'Catalan vengeance.' Fortified at Gallipoli, they crushed Michael IX's army at Apros and for two years systematically devastated Thrace, then moved west to sack the monasteries of Mount Athos and besiege Thessaloniki. No imperial force could meet them in the field.

In 1311 the Catalans marched south, destroyed the Frankish chivalry of Greece at the Halmyros river, and took the Duchy of Athens for themselves, ruling it for seventy years. For the Empire the episode was a double catastrophe: the last field army was squandered, Thrace was left half-desert — and the Turks the Catalans had ferried across the straits had seen how defenseless Europe lay.
detail.zh:
1303年，兵源枯竭的安德洛尼卡二世重金延聘罗杰·德·弗洛尔麾下约六千名久经西西里战事的加泰罗尼亚与阿拉贡老兵。军团在安纳托利亚对突厥人屡有胜绩，却劫掠罗马臣民如同劫掠敌人，其首领的野心更令人不安：罗杰索得“凯撒”尊号，权势近乎独立。1305年4月，他在共治皇帝米海尔九世的行营中遇刺身亡——下手的是阿兰雇佣兵，而皇室至少难逃默许之嫌。

军团的回答是所谓“加泰罗尼亚复仇”。他们据守加里波利，在阿普罗斯击溃米海尔九世的大军，随后两年间将色雷斯蹂躏殆尽，又西进洗劫阿索斯圣山的修道院、围攻塞萨洛尼基。帝国已无一支野战军能与之抗衡。

1311年，加泰罗尼亚人挥师南下，在哈尔米罗斯河畔全歼希腊的法兰克骑士，夺取雅典公国，据有其地达七十年。对帝国而言，这场变乱是双重灾难：最后的野战军就此败光，色雷斯十室九空——而当初被加泰罗尼亚人渡运过海峡的突厥人，也已看清欧洲一侧是何等不设防。

title.en: The Chora Church: Summit of the Palaiologan Renaissance
title.zh: 科拉教堂：帕莱奥洛戈斯文艺复兴的巅峰

summary.en: Between 1316 and 1321 Theodore Metochites rebuilt and adorned the Chora monastery church, whose mosaics and frescoes — above all the Anastasis — mark the highest achievement of late Roman art.
summary.zh: 1316至1321年间，狄奥多尔·梅托希特斯重修并装饰科拉修道院教堂，其马赛克与湿壁画——尤以《基督下降阴间》为最——代表了晚期罗马艺术的最高成就。

detail.en:
The statesman Theodore Metochites poured his fortune into restoring the monastery of Christ in Chora, near the Theodosian walls. The mosaic cycles he commissioned narrate the lives of the Virgin and of Christ in dozens of scenes of astonishing refinement: elongated figures move through swirling architectural fantasies, drapery ripples with inner life, and tender, almost novelistic details — the caress of Joachim and Anna, the midwives bathing the infant Mary — humanize the sacred stories. Above the entrance Metochites himself kneels, in his great turban-like hat, offering the church to Christ.

In the funerary side-chapel, the parekklesion, fresco replaces mosaic and rises to the visionary: the Anastasis in the apse shows Christ in blazing white, striding across the shattered gates of Hades and hauling Adam and Eve bodily from their tombs — perhaps the most powerful single image the Eastern Roman tradition ever produced.

Created while the state crumbled, the Chora decoration proves that the Empire's final artistic flowering was no faint echo but a genuine renaissance: classical in its grace, contemporary in its emotion, and profoundly confident in its theology of resurrection.
detail.zh:
政治家狄奥多尔·梅托希特斯倾其家财，重修了狄奥多西城墙近旁的科拉基督修道院。他出资镶嵌的马赛克组画以数十个场景铺陈圣母与基督的生平，精妙得令人惊叹：修长的人物穿行于盘旋奇诡的建筑幻境之间，衣袂翻卷似有生命，而那些温柔的、近乎小说笔法的细节——约阿希姆与安娜的相拥、稳婆为襁褓中的玛利亚沐浴——让神圣叙事有了人间体温。正门上方，梅托希特斯本人头戴形如缠头的高冠跪坐着，将教堂捧献给基督。

在用作墓室的侧堂里，湿壁画取代马赛克，升华为异象：半穹顶上的《基督下降阴间》中，基督白衣如炽，踏碎地狱之门，将亚当与夏娃从坟墓中奋力拽起——这或许是东罗马艺术传统所创造的最具震撼力的单幅图像。

科拉的装饰完成于国势倾颓之际，却证明帝国最后的艺术繁花绝非余音残响，而是一场真正的复兴：其优雅承自古典，其情感属于当代，其复活神学更透着深沉的自信。

title.en: The Civil War of the Two Andronikoi
title.zh: 两安德洛尼卡内战

summary.en: From 1321 to 1328 the old emperor Andronikos II and his grandson Andronikos III fought an intermittent civil war that drained the treasury, invited Serbian and Bulgarian meddling, and opened the age of Roman self-destruction.
summary.zh: 1321至1328年，老皇帝安德洛尼卡二世与其孙安德洛尼卡三世断续内战，耗空国库，招来塞尔维亚与保加利亚的干预，开启了罗马人自相残杀的时代。

detail.en:
The quarrel began as family tragedy: the dissolute young Andronikos was disinherited by his grandfather after a night ambush meant for a rival killed his own brother instead. Backed by ambitious magnates — above all his brilliant friend John Kantakouzenos — the young man raised Thrace in 1321 by the simple expedient of promising its towns exemption from taxes. Three rounds of war followed, punctuated by hollow reconciliations, with Bulgaria and Serbia each selling support to one side or the other.

In May 1328 Andronikos III entered Constantinople and forced his grandfather to abdicate; the old emperor ended his days as the monk Antonios. The victor proved an energetic soldier, and Kantakouzenos an able chief minister, but the damage ran deep: the fiscal machinery built by the Laskarids was wrecked by wartime tax concessions, the currency slid further, and Anatolia was lost while Roman armies fought Romans.

Most fatally, the war established a pattern — dynastic factions bidding for Serbian, Bulgarian, and soon Turkish arms with grants of Roman land and money — that within a generation would tear the Empire apart beyond repair.
detail.zh:
争端始于一场家门悲剧：放浪的年轻安德洛尼卡派人夜袭情敌，误杀了自己的胞弟，被祖父废黜继承权。这位年轻人在野心勃勃的权贵——尤其是他才华横溢的挚友约翰·坎塔库泽努斯——支持下，于1321年以许诺免税这一简单手段策动色雷斯诸城起事。此后战事三起三落，其间夹杂着貌合神离的和解，保加利亚与塞尔维亚则轮番向交战双方出售支持。

1328年5月，安德洛尼卡三世进入君士坦丁堡，迫使祖父逊位；老皇帝以修士安东尼奥斯之名了却残生。胜利者确是一员干练的战将，坎塔库泽努斯也是称职的首相，但创伤已深入骨髓：拉斯卡利斯王朝苦心搭建的财政体系毁于战时的减税许诺，币值继续下滑，而当罗马人自相攻伐之际，安纳托利亚正在沦丧。

最致命的是，这场战争立下了一个先例——王朝派系以罗马的土地与金钱竞相收买塞尔维亚、保加利亚乃至不久之后突厥人的刀兵。不出一代人，这一模式便将帝国撕裂到无可修补。

title.en: The Universal Judges of the Romans
title.zh: “罗马人总法官”司法改革

summary.en: In 1329 Andronikos III created the katholikoi kritai — four sworn 'universal judges of the Romans' with supreme jurisdiction over all subjects — the last great judicial reform of the Empire.
summary.zh: 1329年，安德洛尼卡三世设立“罗马人总法官”——四名宣誓就职、对全体臣民拥有最高审判权的法官，这是帝国最后一次重大司法改革。

detail.en:
Decades of civil strife had left Roman justice notoriously venal: litigants complained that verdicts went to the highest bidder, and an earlier tribunal of twelve judges created by Andronikos II had quietly collapsed. In 1329 Andronikos III and John Kantakouzenos instituted a new supreme court of four katholikoi kritai ton Rhomaion — 'universal judges of the Romans' — two senior churchmen and two laymen, who swore solemn oaths of incorruptibility, received fixed salaries, and were empowered to judge every subject from the emperor's kin to the poorest peasant, with no appeal against their sentences.

The institution survived an early scandal that might have killed it: in 1337 three of the four judges were convicted of taking bribes and exiled, an act of self-policing that, remarkably, strengthened rather than discredited the court.

Refilled and reformed, the tribunal endured to the very end of the Empire, and provincial versions were installed in Thessaloniki and the Morea. In an age of shrinking frontiers, the universal judges represented something the Romans refused to surrender: the ancient conviction that the Empire was, above all else, a community of law.
detail.zh:
数十年内乱使罗马司法贿赂公行、声名狼藉：诉讼者抱怨判决价高者得，安德洛尼卡二世早年设立的十二人法庭也早已无声无息地瓦解。1329年，安德洛尼卡三世与约翰·坎塔库泽努斯设立由四名“罗马人总法官”组成的新最高法庭——教士与俗人各二，庄严宣誓廉洁奉公，领取固定俸禄，有权审判上自皇亲、下至贫农的一切臣民，其判决不得上诉。

这一制度挺过了一场几乎足以致命的早期丑闻：1337年，四名法官中有三人因受贿定罪流放。这一自我惩戒之举非但没有败坏法庭的名声，反而使其威信更著。

经过补员与整顿，总法官法庭一直延续到帝国的最后时刻，塞萨洛尼基与摩里亚也设立了地方分庭。在疆土日蹙的年代，总法官制度承载着罗马人拒绝放弃的信念：帝国首先是一个法的共同体。

title.en: The Hesychast Controversy and Gregory Palamas
title.zh: 静修派之争与格里高利·帕拉玛斯

summary.en: A decade of fierce theological battle over the monks' vision of divine light ended in 1351 with the triumph of Gregory Palamas's doctrine, which became a defining pillar of Orthodox spirituality.
summary.zh: 围绕修士所见神圣之光的十年激烈神学论战，以1351年格里高利·帕拉玛斯学说的胜利告终，该学说从此成为正教灵修的支柱性教义。

detail.en:
On Mount Athos, monks called hesychasts — 'those who keep stillness' — practiced unceasing inner prayer and claimed that the purified could behold the uncreated light that shone from Christ at his Transfiguration on Tabor. The Calabrian philosopher Barlaam mocked such claims as crude delusion; the Athonite theologian Gregory Palamas answered with a bold distinction: God's essence remains forever unknowable, but his energies — his real operations, including that light — are truly experienced by the saints. Grace, on this view, is nothing less than direct participation in the life of God.

Councils in Constantinople in 1341, 1347, and finally 1351 vindicated Palamas against successive opponents — Barlaam, Akindynos, and the historian Nikephoros Gregoras — though the quarrel became entangled with the civil war, and Palamas himself spent years imprisoned before becoming archbishop of Thessaloniki.

The triumph of hesychasm shaped everything that followed: it fixed the mystical orientation of Orthodox theology, spread with monastic networks to Bulgaria, Serbia, and Russia, and gave the Orthodox world a spiritual identity robust enough to survive the coming centuries without an empire. Palamas was canonized in 1368, within twenty years of his death.
detail.zh:
在阿索斯圣山上，被称为“静修者”的修士们操练不辍的内心祈祷，宣称净化了心灵的人能够亲见基督在塔博尔山显容时放射的非受造之光。卡拉布里亚哲学家巴尔拉姆讥之为粗陋的妄想；圣山神学家格里高利·帕拉玛斯则以一个大胆的区分作答：上帝的本质永不可知，但祂的“能量”——祂真实的运作，包括那道圣光——确能为圣徒所亲历。照此理解，恩典即是对上帝生命的直接分有。

君士坦丁堡先后于1341年、1347年及1351年召开教会会议，帕拉玛斯接连驳倒巴尔拉姆、阿金迪诺斯与史学家格里高拉斯等论敌而获得确认——尽管论战与内战纠缠不清，帕拉玛斯本人也曾系狱多年，后方出任塞萨洛尼基都主教。

静修主义的胜利塑造了此后的一切：它确立了正教神学的神秘主义取向，随修道网络传入保加利亚、塞尔维亚与罗斯，并赋予正教世界一种足以在没有帝国的漫长世纪中存续的精神认同。帕拉玛斯于1368年、即去世不到二十年后被封为圣徒。

title.en: The Great Civil War and the Zealots of Thessaloniki
title.zh: 大内战与塞萨洛尼基狂热派革命

summary.en: The succession war of 1341–47 between John Kantakouzenos and the regency became a social war: in Thessaloniki the revolutionary Zealots expelled the aristocracy and ruled the city as a commune until 1350.
summary.zh: 1341至1347年约翰·坎塔库泽努斯与摄政集团的继位之争演变为一场社会战争：在塞萨洛尼基，革命的狂热派驱逐贵族，以近乎公社的方式统治该城直至1350年。

detail.en:
When Andronikos III died in June 1341, his chief minister John Kantakouzenos claimed the regency for the boy John V, but the empress Anna of Savoy, the patriarch, and the ambitious Alexios Apokaukos declared him a traitor. Proclaimed emperor at Didymoteichon, Kantakouzenos found the towns closing their gates against him — for the war instantly took on the color of class conflict: the landed aristocracy stood with Kantakouzenos, while urban crowds rose against the 'the powerful' everywhere.

Nowhere more than in Thessaloniki, the Empire's second city, where in 1342 a popular faction called the Zealots seized power, drove out or killed the nobles, confiscated aristocratic and monastic wealth, and governed for eight years as a virtually independent commune — the most radical social experiment in Roman history, culminating in the massacre of 1345 when captive aristocrats were hurled from the citadel walls.

Kantakouzenos won the throne in 1347 only by borrowing Turkish armies and marrying his daughter to the emir Orhan; the Zealots held out until 1350. The true victors were foreigners: Stefan Dušan seized Macedonia and had himself crowned 'emperor of the Serbs and Romans,' the Turks learned the roads of Thrace, and the exhausted Empire that emerged was an empire in name alone.
detail.zh:
1341年6月安德洛尼卡三世驾崩，首相约翰·坎塔库泽努斯要求为幼主约翰五世摄政，皇后萨伏依的安娜、牧首与野心家阿莱克修斯·阿波考科斯却宣布他为国贼。坎塔库泽努斯在迪迪莫蒂霍称帝，却发现各城纷纷闭门相拒——因为战争顷刻间染上了阶级冲突的色彩：有地贵族站在坎塔库泽努斯一边，而各地城市民众则群起反抗“权势者”。

帝国第二大城塞萨洛尼基尤为激烈。1342年，号称“狂热派”的平民党人夺取政权，驱逐乃至处死贵族，没收贵族与修道院的财产，以近乎独立公社的方式统治该城八年——这是罗马历史上最激进的社会实验，并在1345年达到顶点：被俘的贵族被从卫城城墙上掷下处死。

坎塔库泽努斯直到1347年才登上皇位，代价是借用突厥大军、并将女儿嫁与埃米尔奥尔汗；狂热派则坚持到1350年。真正的赢家是外人：斯特凡·杜尚攫取马其顿，自加冕为“塞尔维亚人与罗马人的皇帝”，突厥人摸熟了色雷斯的道路，而从废墟中走出的帝国，已只剩下帝国之名。

title.en: The Hexabiblos of Constantine Harmenopoulos
title.zh: 哈尔梅诺普洛斯的《六书》

summary.en: In 1345 the Thessalonian judge Constantine Harmenopoulos completed the Hexabiblos, a six-book digest of Roman law so practical and complete that it remained in legal force in Greece until 1946.
summary.zh: 1345年，塞萨洛尼基法官君士坦丁·哈尔梅诺普洛斯完成《六书》——一部实用而完备的罗马法六卷汇纂，在希腊一直沿用至1946年，方为现代民法典取代。

detail.en:
While Thessaloniki seethed with revolution, one of its magistrates quietly produced the most durable Roman book of the age. Constantine Harmenopoulos, judge and later nomophylax ('guardian of the laws'), distilled the immense inheritance of Roman jurisprudence — the Basilika of Leo VI, the Procheiron, imperial novels, and the working manuals of practicing courts — into six concise books covering procedure, property, obligations, marriage and family, testaments, and penal law, appending the customary building regulations and agrarian rules that everyday disputes actually turned on.

The Hexabiblos aimed not at theory but at usability: a provincial judge could hold in one volume what had once filled sixty. Precisely for that reason it spread everywhere Greek was read, multiplying in manuscripts and, after printing, in editions used across the Orthodox world under Ottoman rule, when church courts kept Roman law alive for a stateless people.

Its afterlife is astonishing: when the modern Greek kingdom was founded in the 1830s, the Hexabiblos was adopted as the civil law of the land, and it was not superseded until the Greek Civil Code of 1946 — six centuries of validity for a handbook written as an empire collapsed.
detail.zh:
当塞萨洛尼基在革命中沸腾之时，该城的一位法官却默默写就了那个时代生命力最长久的罗马典籍。君士坦丁·哈尔梅诺普洛斯——时任法官、后为“法律守护者”——将罗马法学的浩瀚遗产，包括利奥六世的《巴西利卡法典》、《法律手册》、历代新敕以及法庭实务指南，提炼为简明的六卷书，涵盖诉讼程序、物权、债务、婚姻家庭、遗嘱与刑法，并附上日常纠纷真正依据的建筑惯例与农事规则。

《六书》所求不在理论而在实用：一名外省法官从此可将昔日六十卷的内容纳于一册掌中。也正因如此，它传遍一切诵读希腊文之地，抄本层出不穷；印刷术兴起后，其刊本更在奥斯曼统治下的整个正教世界通行——在那个没有国家的年代，教会法庭正是靠它让罗马法为流散之民延续不绝。

它的身后命运令人惊叹：1830年代现代希腊王国建立时，《六书》被采纳为国家民法，直到1946年希腊民法典颁布方告退役——一部写于帝国倾覆之际的手册，竟通行了整整六个世纪。

title.en: The Black Death Strikes Constantinople
title.zh: 黑死病侵袭君士坦丁堡

summary.en: In 1347 plague ships from the Crimea brought the Black Death to Constantinople, killing perhaps a third of its people, gutting trade and taxation, and passing on to devastate the whole Mediterranean.
summary.zh: 1347年，来自克里米亚的疫船将黑死病带到君士坦丁堡，夺去或达三分之一居民的生命，重创商业与税收，并由此蔓延，荼毒整个地中海世界。

detail.en:
In the summer of 1347 Genoese vessels fleeing the siege of Caffa in the Crimea carried the plague bacillus into the Golden Horn. Constantinople thus became the first great city of Europe to meet the Black Death. Mortality was staggering: contemporaries wrote of houses emptied, monasteries extinguished, and ships drifting crewless in the straits. The emperor John VI Kantakouzenos, whose youngest son Andronikos died of the disease, described the epidemic in his memoirs in phrases consciously borrowed from Thucydides' plague of Athens — literature's homage amid ruin.

The economic blow fell on a state already bankrupt from civil war. Population collapse shrank the tax rolls and the rents of what land remained; skilled trades and shipping contracted; and the customs revenue of the capital — already mostly captured by the Genoese colony at Galata across the Horn, which collected several times the dues of the imperial harbor — dwindled further. The court was so poor that at the coronation banquet of 1347, observers noted, the jewels in the diadems were glass.

From Constantinople the pandemic radiated west with the grain ships to Sicily, Genoa, and Marseille. Recurring every decade, the plague ensured that the Empire's last century would be fought out by a population that never recovered.
detail.zh:
1347年夏，从克里米亚卡法之围逃出的热那亚船只，把鼠疫杆菌带进了金角湾。君士坦丁堡由此成为欧洲第一座直面黑死病的大城。死亡触目惊心：时人记述屋舍十室九空，修道院阖院无人，海峡中漂着无人操桨的空船。皇帝约翰六世·坎塔库泽努斯的幼子安德洛尼卡即死于此疫；他在回忆录中描写这场瘟疫时，字句刻意化用修昔底德笔下的雅典大疫——废墟之中，文学犹在致敬。

经济打击落在一个已被内战拖垮的国家身上。人口锐减令税册萎缩、余存土地的地租无着；百工与航运凋敝；而都城的关税收入——本已大半落入金角湾对岸热那亚人的加拉塔殖民地之手，其税入数倍于帝国港口——更形枯竭。宫廷贫窭至此：1347年加冕宴上，观礼者注意到冠冕上的“宝石”竟是玻璃。

大疫随运粮船自君士坦丁堡西传西西里、热那亚与马赛。此后每隔十年左右便卷土重来，帝国的最后一个世纪，注定要由一个再未恢复元气的民族来支撑。

title.en: The Ottomans Seize Gallipoli
title.zh: 奥斯曼人夺取加里波利

summary.en: When an earthquake flattened Gallipoli's walls in March 1354, Ottoman forces under Süleyman Pasha occupied the ruined fortress and refused to leave — the first permanent Turkish foothold in Europe.
summary.zh: 1354年3月，地震震塌加里波利城墙，苏莱曼帕夏率奥斯曼军队占据这座残破要塞并拒不归还——突厥人在欧洲的第一个永久立足点就此形成。

detail.en:
On the night of 1–2 March 1354 a violent earthquake threw down the walls of Gallipoli and the neighboring towns on the European shore of the Hellespont. Süleyman Pasha, son of the emir Orhan, whose troops were already operating in Thrace as allies of John VI Kantakouzenos, moved instantly: he occupied the empty circuit, repaired the fortifications, and shipped over Turkish settlers from Anatolia, declaring that God had given what no sword had taken. Orhan blandly refused his father-in-law Kantakouzenos's offers of money for its return.

The strategic consequence was immense. Gallipoli commanded the narrowest crossing of the straits: with it, the Ottomans possessed a permanent, defensible bridgehead through which armies could pass into Europe at will, immune to the Empire's vanished navy. Raids into Thrace became colonization; within a generation Adrianople itself would fall and become the Ottoman capital.

In Constantinople the news broke Kantakouzenos, whose Turkish alliance now stood exposed as national betrayal; he abdicated within the year and took the monk's habit. The scholar Demetrios Kydones recorded the panic in the City: people asked openly whether it was time to abandon their homes, for the Turks were no longer raiders from beyond the water — they were neighbors inside the gate.
detail.zh:
1354年3月1日至2日夜间，一场强烈地震将赫勒斯滂欧洲一侧的加里波利及邻近城镇的城墙尽数震塌。埃米尔奥尔汗之子苏莱曼帕夏当机立断——其部队本已以约翰六世·坎塔库泽努斯盟军的身份在色雷斯活动——他占据空城，抢修工事，并从安纳托利亚运来突厥移民，宣称这是刀剑未能夺取而真主亲手赐予之地。对于岳父坎塔库泽努斯出钱赎城的提议，奥尔汗淡然回绝。

战略后果极其深远。加里波利扼守海峡最窄的渡口：据有此地，奥斯曼人便拥有了一个永久且可固守的桥头堡，大军可随意进出欧洲，而帝国早已不存在的海军对此无可奈何。对色雷斯的劫掠从此变为殖民；不出一代人，阿德里安堡也将陷落，成为奥斯曼人的都城。

消息传到君士坦丁堡，坎塔库泽努斯声名扫地——他的突厥同盟如今暴露为祸国之举；当年之内他便逊位披上修士袍。学者德米特里奥斯·基多尼斯记下了城中的恐慌：人们公然议论是否该弃家而去，因为突厥人已不再是海那边来的劫掠者——他们是门里的邻居了。

title.en: The Battle of Kosovo, Seen from Constantinople
title.zh: 君士坦丁堡眼中的科索沃战役

summary.en: The mutual slaughter of Kosovo in 1389, where both Sultan Murad I and Prince Lazar died, broke Serbia as a counterweight and left the Empire alone in the tightening grip of the new sultan Bayezid.
summary.zh: 1389年科索沃之战两败俱伤，苏丹穆拉德一世与拉扎尔大公双双殒命；塞尔维亚从此不再是制衡力量，帝国孤悬于新苏丹巴耶济德日益收紧的铁掌之中。

detail.en:
On the field of Kosovo, on 15 June 1389, the army of Prince Lazar of Serbia with its Bosnian allies met Sultan Murad I. The fighting was murderous and its outcome long uncertain; Murad was killed — tradition says by the Serbian knight Miloš Obilić, who feigned desertion — and the captured Lazar was executed in reprisal. Tactically the field was closer to a draw; strategically it was decisive, for the Ottomans could replace their losses and the Serbs could not. Lazar's heirs accepted vassalage, sending tribute and the princess Olivera to the new sultan's court.

From the walls of Constantinople the battle's meaning was unambiguous and grim. The last Balkan power capable of fielding a great army against the Ottomans was gone, and the Empire's encirclement was complete. Worse, the new sultan Bayezid — 'the Thunderbolt' — abandoned his father's patience for open aggrandizement: he summoned the emperor's sons to serve on campaign as vassals, and Manuel II endured the humiliation of helping besiege Philadelphia, the last free Roman city in Asia.

Within five years Bayezid's armies had closed around Constantinople itself, beginning the long blockade of 1394–1402. Kosovo, a battle fought far from Roman soil, had sealed the Roman fate as surely as any defeat of the Empire's own.
detail.zh:
1389年6月15日，塞尔维亚大公拉扎尔及其波斯尼亚盟军在科索沃平原迎战苏丹穆拉德一世。战况惨烈，胜负久悬未决；穆拉德阵中被杀——相传是塞尔维亚骑士米洛什·奥比利奇诈降行刺——被俘的拉扎尔旋即被处决以为报复。就战术而言近乎两败俱伤，就战略而言却是决定性的：奥斯曼人补得上损失，塞尔维亚人补不上。拉扎尔的后嗣接受藩属地位，向新苏丹的宫廷输送贡赋，并献上公主奥莉薇拉。

从君士坦丁堡城头望去，这场战役的意义清晰而冷峻：巴尔干最后一支能与奥斯曼人堂堂对阵的力量消失了，帝国的合围就此完成。更糟的是，新苏丹巴耶济德——绰号“雷霆”——抛弃了乃父的耐心，转向公开的扩张：他征召皇帝诸子以藩臣身份随军出征，曼努埃尔二世被迫忍辱协攻费拉德尔菲亚——罗马人在亚洲最后一座自由城市。

不出五年，巴耶济德的大军便合围君士坦丁堡本城，开始了1394至1402年的长期封锁。科索沃之战虽远在罗马国土之外，却如帝国自身的任何一场败仗一样，注定了罗马的命运。

title.en: Manuel II's Journey to the West
title.zh: 曼努埃尔二世的西行之旅

summary.en: With Constantinople strangled by Bayezid's blockade, Manuel II traveled to Venice, Paris, and London in 1399–1403 to beg for aid — the first reigning Roman emperor ever to visit England or France.
summary.zh: 君士坦丁堡困于巴耶济德的封锁之际，曼努埃尔二世于1399至1403年远赴威尼斯、巴黎与伦敦求援——他是历史上首位到访英法的在位罗马皇帝。

detail.en:
In December 1399, leaving his nephew as regent in a starving, half-besieged capital, Manuel II Palaiologos sailed west to plead the Empire's cause in person. His progress was a spectacle Europe never forgot: the heir of Constantine, dignified, learned, and dressed in unvarying white, was feasted by the doge in Venice, welcomed by Charles VI into Paris with royal honors, and hosted at Christmas of 1400 by Henry IV at Eltham Palace near London, where the English marveled that so great a prince had come so far as a supplicant. A London lawyer wrote with sorrow of 'this great Christian prince, driven by the infidel from the furthest East to these furthest islands of the West.'

Manuel used the years well — he lodged scholars, discussed theology with Paris masters, and wrote elegant letters home — but gathered promises rather than armies; the West, riven by schism and the Hundred Years War, sent little.

Salvation came from the opposite direction: in July 1402 Timur annihilated Bayezid's army at Ankara and took the sultan captive. The blockade dissolved, and Manuel returned home in 1403 to an empire reprieved — its lands partly restored by squabbling Ottoman princes, its doom postponed by half a century.
detail.zh:
1399年12月，曼努埃尔二世·帕莱奥洛戈斯把饥馑围城中的都城托付侄儿摄政，亲自扬帆西行，为帝国陈情。他的行程成为欧洲久久难忘的盛景：这位君士坦丁的传人举止庄重、学识渊深、常年一袭白衣，在威尼斯受执政官盛宴款待，在巴黎得查理六世以王礼相迎，1400年圣诞则由亨利四世在伦敦近郊的埃尔特姆宫接待。英格兰人惊叹，如此伟大的君主竟以求援者之身远道而来；一位伦敦法律家伤感地写道：“这位伟大的基督徒君主，被异教徒从最遥远的东方，一路逼到我们西方最遥远的岛屿。”

曼努埃尔没有虚度这几年——他礼遇学者，与巴黎的学界大家论道神学，并写下文辞优雅的家书——然而带回的是承诺而非军队；深陷教会分裂与百年战争的西方，实际驰援寥寥。

拯救来自相反的方向：1402年7月，帖木儿在安卡拉全歼巴耶济德的大军并生俘苏丹。封锁瓦解，曼努埃尔于1403年回到一个获得缓刑的帝国——奥斯曼诸王子内讧之际部分失地得以归还，帝国的末日被推迟了半个世纪。

title.en: The Flowering of the Morea and Mistra
title.zh: 摩里亚的兴盛与米斯特拉斯

summary.en: In the early fifteenth century the Despotate of the Morea became the Empire's most vital province, and its capital Mistra — a hillside city of palaces, churches, and libraries — its last great cultural center.
summary.zh: 十五世纪初，摩里亚君主国成为帝国最富生机的行省，其首府米斯特拉斯——一座宫殿、教堂与藏书楼层叠的山城——则是帝国最后一个伟大的文化中心。

detail.en:
While Constantinople decayed within its walls, the Peloponnese ran against the current of decline. Governed from Mistra by despots of the imperial house — sons and brothers of emperors — the Morea steadily pushed back the remnants of Frankish rule until, by 1430, almost the whole peninsula was Roman again: the Empire's only successful reconquest of its final century. The despots rebuilt the Hexamilion wall across the Isthmus of Corinth in hope of sealing the province against Ottoman raids.

Mistra itself, cascading down a spur of Mount Taygetos above the vale of Sparta, grew into a true capital in miniature: the palace of the despots with its great vaulted throne hall, mansions of the archons, and a constellation of churches — the Peribleptos, the Hodegetria, the metropolis of St. Demetrios — whose frescoes carried Palaiologan painting to its final refinement.

Scholars, jurists, and artists gathered where patronage still lived; the philosopher Plethon made the city famous across Europe. It was fitting that the last emperor came from this last flowering: Constantine XI, despot at Mistra, was crowned in its cathedral in January 1449 before sailing to his capital and his fate.
detail.zh:
当君士坦丁堡在城墙之内日渐凋敝，伯罗奔尼撒却逆流而上。摩里亚由皇族出身的君主——历代皇帝的子弟——坐镇米斯特拉斯治理，稳步收复法兰克残余势力的领地；至1430年，几乎整个半岛重归罗马——这是帝国最后一个世纪里唯一成功的收复失地之举。诸君主还重修了横跨科林斯地峡的六里长墙，冀图将行省隔绝于奥斯曼人的侵袭之外。

米斯特拉斯本身沿泰格特斯山的支脉层层而下，俯瞰斯巴达河谷，俨然一座微缩的都城：君主宫殿有恢宏的拱顶正殿，执政贵族的宅邸错落其间，更有群星般的教堂——佩里夫勒普托斯、指路圣母堂、圣德米特里主教座堂——其湿壁画将帕莱奥洛戈斯绘画推向最后的精纯。

凡赞助犹存之处，学者、法学家与艺术家便会聚而来；哲人普莱东更令此城名动全欧。末代皇帝出自这最后的繁花，可谓恰如其分：君士坦丁十一世曾任米斯特拉斯君主，1449年1月在城中主教座堂加冕，随后扬帆驶向他的都城，也驶向他的命运。

title.en: The Pantanassa Frescoes at Mistra
title.zh: 米斯特拉斯潘塔纳萨教堂壁画

summary.en: Consecrated in 1428, the church of the Pantanassa at Mistra received a fresco cycle of luminous color and festive vitality — the last major monument of Eastern Roman painting.
summary.zh: 1428年祝圣的米斯特拉斯潘塔纳萨教堂，绘有色彩明丽、气象生动的壁画组画——这是东罗马绘画最后一座重要的丰碑。

detail.en:
The church of the Virgin Pantanassa — 'Queen of All' — was founded by John Frangopoulos, chief minister of the Despotate of the Morea, and consecrated in September 1428 on a terrace high above the Eurotas valley. Its architecture is itself a summation, blending the domed cross-in-square tradition with Gothic touches learned from the Franks: a pointed-arched belfry and lacy carved ornament that would not look out of place in Italy.

The frescoes of the upper registers, painted around the time of consecration, are the swan song of Roman monumental art. In scenes such as the Annunciation, the Nativity, the Raising of Lazarus, and the Entry into Jerusalem, painters of astonishing freshness piled up fantastic mountains and cities, filled the crowds with individual faces, and keyed the whole to radiant rose, turquoise, and gold — a palette more joyous than anything the age's politics deserved.

Created twenty-five years before the fall of Constantinople, the Pantanassa cycle shows a tradition still inventing at the very edge of extinction. The monastery, alone in Mistra, has remained inhabited by nuns ever since — a living thread back to the world that painted it.
detail.zh:
万后圣母教堂——“潘塔纳萨”意为“万有之后”——由摩里亚君主国首相约翰·弗兰戈普洛斯创建，1428年9月在俯瞰欧罗塔斯河谷的高台上祝圣。其建筑本身便是一部集大成之作：穹顶内接十字式的传统之上，融入了从法兰克人那里习得的哥特意趣——尖拱钟楼与镂空雕饰，置于意大利亦不显突兀。

上层壁画约绘于祝圣前后，堪称罗马纪念性艺术的天鹅之歌。在《圣母领报》《基督诞生》《拉撒路复活》《进入耶路撒冷》诸场景中，画师们以惊人的清新笔意堆叠出奇崛的山峦与城郭，让人群中的每张面孔各具神情，通幅统摄于明艳的玫瑰红、松石绿与金色之中——这样欢悦的色调，远非那个时代的政局所配。

潘塔纳萨组画完成于君士坦丁堡陷落前二十五年，证明这一传统直到覆灭的边缘仍在创造。这座修道院是米斯特拉斯唯一延续至今的活修院，修女们世代居于其中——一缕活着的丝线，仍系着绘下它的那个世界。

title.en: Gemistos Plethon and the Platonic Revival at Mistra
title.zh: 格弥斯托斯·普莱东与米斯特拉斯的柏拉图复兴

summary.en: At Mistra the philosopher George Gemistos Plethon taught Plato, drafted radical plans to save the Peloponnese, and inspired the Platonic revival that his Florentine admirers carried into the Italian Renaissance.
summary.zh: 哲学家格弥斯托斯·普莱东在米斯特拉斯讲授柏拉图，起草拯救伯罗奔尼撒的激进方略，并点燃了柏拉图复兴之火——他的佛罗伦萨仰慕者将这把火种带入意大利文艺复兴。

detail.en:
George Gemistos, who styled himself Plethon in homage to Plato, settled at Mistra around 1410 as a judge and teacher, and for four decades made the little capital of the Morea the most original intellectual center in the Greek world. In memoranda addressed to the emperor Manuel II and the despot Theodore he proposed a startlingly radical reconstruction: a native standing army in place of mercenaries, land reform, and a self-sufficient Peloponnesian state — appealing not to Roman tradition but to the ancient Hellenic descent of its people, an early cry of Hellenic identity.

In private he went much further. His Book of Laws, shown only to initiates, sketched a philosophical religion of the Olympian gods understood as Platonic principles; after his death the scandalized patriarch Gennadios burned it, and only fragments survive.

His European fame was made at the Council of Florence in 1438–39, where, sidelined from theological debate, the old man lectured Italian humanists on the differences between Plato and Aristotle. Cosimo de' Medici, Ficino later wrote, conceived from those lectures the idea of a Platonic Academy in Florence. When Plethon died at Mistra, Italian devotees stole his bones so that 'the great teacher might rest among free men' — in the Tempio Malatestiano at Rimini.
detail.zh:
乔治·格弥斯托斯以“普莱东”自号，以致敬柏拉图。约1410年起，他以法官与教师之身定居米斯特拉斯，四十年间将这座摩里亚小都城打造成希腊世界最具原创性的思想中心。在呈交皇帝曼努埃尔二世与君主狄奥多尔的奏议中，他提出了惊人激进的重建方案：以本土常备军取代雇佣兵，推行土地改革，建立自给自足的伯罗奔尼撒邦国——其立论不诉诸罗马传统，而诉诸这片土地上人民古老的希腊血脉，堪称希腊认同最早的呐喊之一。

私下里他走得更远。仅示于入室弟子的《法律书》勾画了一种哲学宗教，把奥林波斯诸神诠释为柏拉图式的本原；他身后，震怒的牧首根纳迪奥斯将其付之一炬，今仅存残篇。

他的欧洲声名成就于1438至1439年的佛罗伦萨大公会议：被排除在神学论辩之外的老人，转而向意大利人文主义者讲授柏拉图与亚里士多德之异同。费奇诺后来写道，正是这些讲论使科西莫·德·美第奇萌生了在佛罗伦萨创办柏拉图学园的念头。普莱东逝于米斯特拉斯后，意大利的崇拜者竟盗走他的遗骨，安厝于里米尼的马拉泰斯塔神庙——“好让伟大的导师安息在自由人中间”。

title.en: The Council of Florence and the Union of the Churches
title.zh: 佛罗伦萨大公会议与两教会合一

summary.en: In July 1439 Emperor John VIII and the Greek delegation signed the union of the Eastern and Western churches at Florence in exchange for promised aid — a union the people of Constantinople never accepted.
summary.zh: 1439年7月，皇帝约翰八世率希腊代表团在佛罗伦萨签署东西教会合一法令，以换取西方许诺的援助——而君士坦丁堡的民众始终拒绝承认这一合一。

detail.en:
In 1437 John VIII Palaiologos staked everything on a last diplomatic gamble: he sailed to Italy with the aged patriarch Joseph II and some seven hundred clergy and scholars to negotiate the healing of the four-century schism, hoping union would unlock a Western crusade against the Ottomans. After a year of grinding debate at Ferrara the council moved to Florence, where Medici money sustained it. The Greek delegation contained the best minds of the dying Empire — Bessarion of Nicaea and Isidore of Kiev arguing for union, Mark Eugenikos of Ephesus immovably against — and Plethon, whose lectures on Plato electrified the humanists.

On 6 July 1439 the decree Laetentur Caeli was read in Greek and Latin beneath Brunelleschi's new dome: the Greeks accepted the Filioque, purgatory, and papal primacy, with their rites preserved. Patriarch Joseph had died weeks earlier; Mark of Ephesus alone refused to sign, and the pope is said to have murmured, 'Then we have accomplished nothing.'

So it proved. Constantinople's people and most of its clergy repudiated the union as bought with the Empire's soul; the promised crusade died at Varna in 1444; and the union was formally proclaimed in Hagia Sophia only in December 1452 — five months before the end, in a city that famously muttered it preferred the sultan's turban to the cardinal's hat.
detail.zh:
1437年，约翰八世·帕莱奥洛戈斯押上一切，作最后一次外交豪赌：他偕年迈的牧首约瑟夫二世及约七百名教士学者渡海赴意大利，谈判弥合四个世纪的教会分裂，指望以合一换取西方对奥斯曼人的十字军。会议先在费拉拉经年苦辩，后迁至佛罗伦萨，由美第奇家族的财力维持。希腊代表团汇集了垂亡帝国最杰出的头脑——尼西亚的贝萨里翁与基辅的伊西多尔力主合一，以弗所的马可·尤金尼科斯坚不让步——还有普莱东，他关于柏拉图的讲论令人文主义者如痴如醉。

1439年7月6日，在布鲁内莱斯基新落成的穹顶之下，《诸天欢欣》法令以希腊文与拉丁文宣读：希腊一方接受“和子句”、炼狱说与教宗首席权，礼仪得以保留。牧首约瑟夫已于数周前病逝；唯有以弗所的马可拒绝签字，据说教宗闻讯喃喃道：“那我们便一事无成。”

果然如此。君士坦丁堡的民众与多数教士唾弃这场以帝国灵魂作价的合一；许诺的十字军1444年折戟瓦尔纳；而合一法令直到1452年12月才在圣索菲亚大教堂正式宣读——距离末日仅五个月。城中流传的那句名言是：宁见苏丹的头巾，不见枢机的红帽。

title.en: Mehmed II Builds Rumeli Hisarı
title.zh: 穆罕默德二世修筑鲁梅利堡垒

summary.en: In the spring and summer of 1452 Mehmed II built the fortress of Rumeli Hisarı on Roman soil at the Bosporus's narrowest point, cutting Constantinople's lifeline to the Black Sea and announcing the coming siege.
summary.zh: 1452年春夏，穆罕默德二世在博斯普鲁斯海峡最窄处的罗马国土上筑起鲁梅利堡垒，掐断君士坦丁堡通往黑海的生命线，末日之围的号角就此吹响。

detail.en:
The young sultan Mehmed II, nineteen at his accession, made no secret of his obsession with the City. In April 1452 his workmen began raising a great fortress on the European shore of the Bosporus, directly opposite the castle his great-grandfather had built on the Asian side — on land that was still, in law, the emperor's. Thousands of masons and laborers, with the sultan himself supervising and his viziers each financing a tower, completed the huge triangular work in a mere four and a half months. The Turks called it Boğazkesen — 'cutter of the strait,' or, with equal accuracy, 'cutter of the throat.'

Its guns commanded the narrowest four hundred meters of the channel, and the sultan decreed that every passing ship must stop for inspection and toll. That November a Venetian galley sailing down from the Black Sea refused: it was sunk by a single stone shot, and its captain, Antonio Rizzo, was impaled — a message legible in every chancery in Europe.

Constantine XI's protests were returned with a declaration of war in all but name. Grain from the Black Sea could no longer reach the City. The Empire's capital was now sealed from both seas, and everyone, in Constantinople and beyond, understood that the final act had begun.
detail.zh:
年方十九即位的年轻苏丹穆罕默德二世，从不掩饰他对这座城的执念。1452年4月，他的工匠开始在博斯普鲁斯海峡的欧洲岸边兴筑一座巨型要塞，与其曾祖父在亚洲一侧所建的城堡隔水相对——而这片土地在法理上仍属皇帝。数千名石匠与役夫昼夜赶工，苏丹亲临督造，诸维齐尔各自出资承建一座塔楼，这座庞大的三角形要塞仅用四个半月便告落成。突厥人称之为“博阿兹凯森”——“断峡者”，或者同样贴切地说，“断喉者”。

堡中巨炮扼守海峡最窄的四百米水道，苏丹下令过往船只一律停船受检、缴纳通行税。当年11月，一艘自黑海南下的威尼斯桨帆船拒不从命：一发石弹便将其击沉，船长安东尼奥·里佐被处以穿刺之刑——这条讯息，欧洲每一个宫廷都读得明白。

君士坦丁十一世的抗议换来的实际上是宣战书。黑海的粮食从此再也运不进城。帝国的都城已被两面海路封死；无论城里城外，人人都明白：最后一幕开场了。

title.en: The Fall of Constantinople and the Last Stand of Constantine XI
title.zh: 君士坦丁堡的陷落与君士坦丁十一世的最后一战

summary.en: After a fifty-three-day siege, Mehmed II's army stormed Constantinople on 29 May 1453; Constantine XI died fighting at the breach, and the Roman Empire, after more than two thousand years, came to its end.
summary.zh: 历经五十三天的围攻，穆罕默德二世的大军于1453年5月29日攻破君士坦丁堡；君士坦丁十一世战死于城墙缺口，绵延两千余年的罗马国祚就此终结。

detail.en:
On 6 April 1453 Mehmed II drew up perhaps eighty thousand men before the Theodosian walls, with a navy in the straits and the monstrous bombard of the engineer Urban, whose stone shot weighed half a ton. Against them Constantine XI could muster fewer than eight thousand defenders — Romans, Venetians, and the Genoese condottiere Giovanni Giustiniani Longo — to man fourteen miles of fortification. For seven weeks the ancient walls, ruined by day, were rebuilt by night. When the chain across the Golden Horn balked his fleet, Mehmed hauled seventy ships overland on greased timbers into the harbor — a stroke that stretched the defense past its limit. A last Venetian brig slipped through the blockade and returned with word that no relief fleet was coming: the City stood alone.

The final assault came before dawn on Tuesday, 29 May. Two waves broke against the stockade at the Gate of St. Romanos; the third, the janissaries, attacked as Giustiniani, gravely wounded, was carried to the ships and the defense wavered. Turks poured through the breach and the small sally-port of the Kerkoporta. Constantine XI, refusing all urging to flee, tore off the imperial insignia and charged into the melee with his companions; his body was never certainly found. By tradition the emperor's last words were that the City was taken but he still lived — and then he was gone into the fighting.

Mehmed gave his soldiers the customary three days of plunder, then halted the sack and entered Hagia Sophia, where the muezzin's call replaced the liturgy that had sounded there for nine hundred years. Refugees' ships carried the news west, and Christendom grieved as for a world ended. An empire that traced its line from Augustus through Constantine — 1,123 years from the city's dedication — had fallen; the story that began on the Bosporus in 330 closed there, in fire, on a May morning.
detail.zh:
1453年4月6日，穆罕默德二世将约八万大军列阵于狄奥多西城墙之前，舰队封锁海峡，工程师乌尔班铸造的巨炮随军而至——其石弹重达半吨。与之相对，君士坦丁十一世能召集的守军不足八千人——罗马人、威尼斯人，以及热那亚雇佣军统帅乔万尼·朱斯蒂尼亚尼·隆戈——却要据守二十余公里的城防。七个星期里，千年古墙白日倾颓，入夜重筑。金角湾的铁链挡住了苏丹的舰队，穆罕默德便命人在涂满油脂的木轨上把七十艘战船拖过陆地送入湾内——这一着奇兵，将守军拉扯到了极限之外。一艘威尼斯双桅船最后一次突破封锁侦察归来，带回的消息是：没有援军舰队。这座城，只剩下它自己。

总攻发起于5月29日星期二的黎明之前。前两波攻势碎裂在圣罗曼努斯门的栅垒之下；第三波是耶尼切里军团——恰在此时，身负重伤的朱斯蒂尼亚尼被抬往船上，防线随之动摇。突厥人从缺口与科克波塔小门蜂拥而入。君士坦丁十一世拒绝了一切劝逃之声，扯下皇帝仪服的徽记，与身边同伴一道冲入乱军之中；他的遗体从未被确证寻获。相传皇帝的遗言是：城已陷落，而我还活着——言毕便消失在战阵之中。

穆罕默德依惯例许士卒劫掠三日，随后下令止掠，进入圣索菲亚大教堂；在礼赞声回荡了九百年的穹顶之下，响起了宣礼人的召唤。难民的船只把消息带往西方，整个基督教世界如丧考妣。这个自奥古斯都经君士坦丁一脉相承的帝国——距都城奉献之日已一千一百二十三年——就此倾覆；公元330年在博斯普鲁斯海峡开始的故事，在五月的一个清晨，于烈火之中，在同一片海峡边落幕。

title.en: The Scholars Carry Greek Learning to the West
title.zh: 学者西迁，希腊学问传入西方

summary.en: With the Empire's fall, its scholars and their manuscripts streamed to Italy, where émigrés such as Bessarion, Argyropoulos, and Chalkokondyles fed the Renaissance with the Greek learning the Romans had guarded for a millennium.
summary.zh: 帝国倾覆后，学者们携抄本流亡意大利；贝萨里翁、阿吉罗普洛斯、哈尔科孔迪利斯等流亡者，把罗马人守护千年的希腊学问注入了文艺复兴。

detail.en:
The fall of Constantinople scattered the custodians of Greek learning across the Mediterranean, and the current ran overwhelmingly toward Italy, where two generations of contact — Manuel Chrysoloras's Florentine Greek lessons of the 1390s, the delegations of Ferrara-Florence — had prepared eager patrons. Now the trickle became a migration: teachers, copyists, and refugee families landed at Venice and Crete carrying what they could save, and what they could save was, above all, books.

Cardinal Bessarion, the convert of Florence, made himself the nation's librarian in exile, spending his fortune to gather more than a thousand codices — 'lest the Greeks be so utterly stripped that no trace of their letters remain' — and bequeathed them in 1468 to Venice, where they became the core of the Biblioteca Marciana. John Argyropoulos taught Aristotle in Florence to pupils including the young Lorenzo de' Medici; Demetrios Chalkokondyles brought the first printed Homer through the press in 1488; Constantine Lascaris's Greek grammar was the first book printed entirely in Greek. Around the Aldine press in Venice, émigrés soon turned the whole classical inheritance into print.

It is too simple to say the Empire's death caused the Renaissance — the traffic had begun earlier. But the finality of 1453 turned transmission into translation of a civilization: the Roman East, losing its state, gave its learning to Europe, and Plato, Homer, and the Greek Testament entered the Western mind as the Empire's last bequest.
detail.zh:
君士坦丁堡的陷落使希腊学问的守护者们流散于地中海各处，而人流压倒性地涌向意大利——此前两代人的交往已在那里备好了热切的赞助者：1390年代曼努埃尔·赫里索洛拉斯在佛罗伦萨开讲希腊文，费拉拉—佛罗伦萨会议的使团又续其绪。此刻涓流化作迁徙大潮：教师、抄工与流亡家庭在威尼斯与克里特登岸，带着他们抢救出的一切——而他们抢救出的，首先是书。

佛罗伦萨会议上皈依的枢机贝萨里翁，甘为流亡民族的图书馆员，倾其家财搜求千余部抄本——“以免希腊人被剥夺至尽，连其文字的痕迹都荡然无存”——并于1468年将其悉数遗赠威尼斯，成为马尔恰纳图书馆的馆藏之基。约翰·阿吉罗普洛斯在佛罗伦萨讲授亚里士多德，弟子中有年轻的洛伦佐·德·美第奇；德米特里奥斯·哈尔科孔迪利斯于1488年主持刊印了史上第一部荷马印本；君士坦丁·拉斯卡利斯的希腊文法则是第一部全希腊文印刷的书籍。不久，流亡者们又聚集在威尼斯的阿尔杜斯印坊周围，把整个古典遗产付诸铅字。

说帝国之死催生了文艺复兴，未免过于简单——这场传递早已开始。但1453年的终局，使知识的传递升华为一个文明的移译：罗马的东方失去了国家，却把学问赠予了欧洲。柏拉图、荷马与希腊文圣经进入西方心灵，成为帝国最后的遗产。
